nativeRange.habitat: Coral reefs and rocky outcrops, typically found at depths of 2–175 m; most commonly observed between 15–75 m

diet.notes: Generally offered a varied diet that tends to include small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and chopped clams, as well as herbivore preparations. In the wild, juveniles have been documented cleaning parasites from other fish at cleaning stations. Feeding several times daily is commonly advised given the species' high activity level.

avoidWith: Chaetodon butterflyfishes that may compete for food

summary: A striking butterflyfish with bold black-and-white banding and a long white dorsal filament. Generally considered peaceful and relatively hardy; tends to do best in larger tanks with ample open swimming space and a varied meaty diet.
careNotes:
Heniochus acuminatus is generally regarded as one of the more forgiving butterflyfish to keep, though it tends to benefit from a well-established, larger aquarium. The elongated white dorsal filament — a distinctive feature of the species — streams in the current as the fish swims. Adults are commonly observed in pairs in the wild; sourcing a bonded pair or introducing two individuals simultaneously has been reported to improve long-term cohabitation.

This species is not generally considered reef safe: it has been documented nipping at tubeworms, soft corals, large-polyp stony corals, and Tridacna clam mantles. It is better suited to a fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) setup.

Juveniles in the wild have been observed acting as cleaner fish at cleaning stations, picking ectoparasites from other fish — a behavior that typically diminishes as they mature. Juveniles sold in the trade tend to acclimate well and generally accept prepared foods more readily than many other butterflyfish species.

Temperature: sources range from 22–29°C; a working target of 23–27°C averages the aquarium-hobby recommendations (LiveAquaria 22–25.6°C) with FishBase wild-distribution data (23.6–29.1°C). Adult size: FishBase records a maximum of 25 cm TL; aquarium sources commonly report 15–22 cm in captivity. Tank size: recommendations range from ~280 L (75 gal) to 500 L (132 gal); 500 L is used here, biased toward the larger end given the species' active swimming behavior and preference for open water.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has been rarely documented. In the wild the species is oviparous; spawning typically occurs at dusk in pairs near the reef surface. Successfully inducing spawning and rearing pelagic larvae in home aquariums is considered very difficult.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 25 cm TL), temperature range (23.6–29.1°C), native range, and taxonomy. Temperature averaged with LiveAquaria values to produce 23–27°C working range.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 25 cm, SG 1.026, minimum tank 500 L, reef-unsafe status (nips tubeworms, soft corals, Tridacna). Tank size recommendation of 500 L used as primary sizing reference given active swimming noted.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic spawner; rarely if ever bred successfully in home aquariums. Wild pairs spawn near the reef surface at dusk.
